tree.title: What should I listen to right now?
tree.description: A decision tree to help you find the right music for the moment.

branch.content: What are you doing right now?
branch.fork: Moving — working out or driving
Socialising — with people
At home with headphones
Working or focusing

branch.content: Working out or driving?
branch.fork: Working out
Driving

branch.content: Do you want music to push you, or something to zone out to?
branch.fork: Push me, I need the aggression
Zone out, lock in and disappear

branch.content: Raw and heavy, or fast and precise?
branch.fork: Raw and heavy — I want to feel it
Fast and technical — surgical

branch.content: Noisia on FabricLive 40. Relentless neurofunk with a physical weight to it. Or the Dj St!cky Freeparty Filth series if you want something more stripped and industrial.
Ed Rush and Optical. Virus Vaults or the Wormhole album. Neurofunk as precision engineering — cold, clinical, unforgiving. Perfect for when you want your head somewhere else entirely.
Rolling DnB or hypnotic groove?
branch.fork: Rolling DnB, steady forward momentum
Hypnotic groove, something more minimal

branch.content: Seba. His mixes have that half-step roll that keeps you moving without demanding your attention. The 70-minute mix is a good starting point. Autonomic era DnB at its most effortless.
Luomo. Vocalcity. Finnish minimal house — warm, understated, locks you into a rhythm without you noticing. Odd choice for a workout but it genuinely works if you just want to be in your own world.
Long journey or short hop?
branch.fork: Long journey, need something that sustains
Short hop, just need something for now

branch.content: Do you want a mix to run unattended, or something familiar you already know well?
branch.fork: A good mix, set it and forget it
Something familiar I can half-focus on

branch.content: What mood for the road?
branch.fork: Energised — keep me alert and moving
Mellow — smooth and sustained

branch.content: FabricLive 03 with DJ Hype for jump-up energy, or FabricLive 40 with Noisia if you want something darker and more technical. Both will keep you focused across a long drive.
FabricLive 46 with LTJ Bukem. Atmospheric DnB that flows across an entire journey without interrupting your thoughts. Or FabricLive 68 Calibre if you want something a shade warmer.
Electronic or hip hop?
branch.fork: Electronic, something I know well
Hip hop, familiar and comfortable

branch.content: Amon Tobin on a long drive is genuinely special. Out From Out Where or Supermodified. Music that shifts and evolves exactly as a journey does — you arrive somewhere different to where you started.
Beastie Boys — Paul's Boutique. An album that rewards familiarity. You catch something new every time, and it is dense enough to carry a long drive without getting stale.
Want something to pump you up, or just fill the time?
branch.fork: Pump me up for what I am about to do
Just fill the journey, nothing specific

branch.content: Deekline and Ed Solo or DJ Craze. Short, punchy, high energy. Breaks with attitude — you will arrive already in the right headspace.
Pick a single De La Soul or Beastie Boys track and let it shuffle. Nothing better than a good song you know well filling a ten-minute drive.
A few people or a bigger group?
branch.fork: Small group — a few people
Bigger group — proper gathering

branch.content: Do you know their taste, or is it mixed company?
branch.fork: I know what they are into
Mixed company, different tastes

branch.content: Are they into electronic music or broader?
branch.fork: They know the scene, we are all on the same page
Broader taste — not a scene crowd

branch.content: Are people likely to dance, or are you listening and talking?
branch.fork: Dancing is likely
Listening, appreciating, talking about it

branch.content: Stanton Warriors, Freestylers, or Deekline and Ed Solo. High energy breaks that get a room moving without being overwhelming. Credible but accessible for a small space.
Amon Tobin, Four Tet FabricLive 59, or Clams Casino instrumentals. Music that rewards close attention and gives people something to actually talk about. Put it on and let the conversation follow.
Is there a common ground genre, or do you want to try something new on them?
branch.fork: Use hip hop as common ground
We are adventurous, try something different

branch.content: De La Soul or Beastie Boys. Both are accessible enough for people who do not know the scene but interesting enough that they will want to know more. Stakes Is High or Paul's Boutique.
DJ Aphrodite melodic mix. It is the best gateway drug for DnB sceptics — smooth enough to not alienate anyone, interesting enough to hook people who have never heard the genre. Works every time.
Do you want something that works as background but rewards attention, or do you want to get people moving?
branch.fork: Background that rewards attention
Get people moving

branch.content: Clams Casino instrumentals or Luomo. Interesting enough to notice, unobtrusive enough to talk over. The kind of music that people ask about halfway through the evening.
Breaks or UK Garage?
branch.fork: Breaks — big and energetic
UK Garage or Bassline — groovy and rhythmic

branch.content: Freestylers or Stanton Warriors. Punchy UK breaks with enough swagger to get anyone moving, whatever their background. Hard to resist.
DJ Q on FabricLive 99, or the Nkogliaz Speed Garage mix. That UK Garage groove is infectious in a small space. The tempo is just right for a room that is warming up.
Party with dancing, or a gathering where music is in the background?
branch.fork: Party — people are there to dance
Gathering — music in the background

branch.content: Does the crowd know the scene, or is it mixed?
branch.fork: They know — play what you actually like
Mixed crowd — needs to work for everyone

branch.content: Build it properly. Start with Aphrodite or DJ Marky to warm the room up, move into DJ Hype or Noisia when it is going, then close with DJ Craze for the set you actually want to play. Earn the heavy stuff.
Aphrodite melodic, Freestylers, or Stanton Warriors. Accessible but credible — electronic music that a mixed crowd will respond to without you having to compromise your taste too badly.
Electronic or hip hop as the background?
branch.fork: Electronic — textural and interesting
Hip hop — familiar and easy

branch.content: Amon Tobin or Four Tet FabricLive 59. Complex enough to be interesting if anyone tunes in, unobtrusive enough to disappear into the room. Sounds like you put thought into the playlist, which you did.
De La Soul and Beastie Boys back to back. Warm, familiar, no rough edges. Everyone has a loose connection to it even if they cannot name the tracks. Safe choice in the best possible sense.
What time of day is it?
branch.fork: Morning or afternoon
Evening
Late night

branch.content: Are you actively listening or is it background for something else?
branch.fork: Actively listening — this is the activity
Background for something else

branch.content: What do you want from it?
branch.fork: Complexity — something with layers to unpick
Emotion — something that actually moves me
Discovery — hear something I have not heard before

branch.content: Structural complexity or sonic complexity?
branch.fork: Structural — I want intricate construction
Sonic — I want incredible production craft

branch.content: Amon Tobin — Out From Out Where. Every track is a puzzle built from unlikely samples. Put headphones on, close your eyes, and try to work out where the sounds are coming from. You probably cannot.
Noisia — FabricLive 40. Production technique as art form. The way they layer, compress, and sculpt sound is unlike anything else in DnB. Worth headphone listening purely to study the craft.
What kind of emotion?
branch.fork: Nostalgic — that warm familiar feeling
Searching — something more open and melancholic

branch.content: De La Soul — 3 Feet High and Rising, or Beastie Boys — Paul's Boutique. Both are dense with detail that rewards the kind of close listening you only do with headphones on. Golden era hip hop at its most generous.

Calibre or LTJ Bukem. FabricLive 68 or Logical Progression. The kind of liquid DnB that sounds like it is asking a question it cannot quite answer. Put it on when you have something on your mind and let it do the work.
Somewhere familiar but deeper, or something genuinely different?
branch.fork: Familiar territory but deeper — artist I know, album I have not heard
Something genuinely different — take me somewhere new

branch.content: Pick an artist you already respect and go to their earliest or most obscure record. Cujo — Adventures In Foam is a good example: Shadow Records-era DnB that sounds nothing like what you expect. The gaps in a discography are usually where the interesting stuff hides.

The Bug — Fire or London Zoo. It connects DnB, dancehall, grime, and industrial in a way that sounds like nothing else. Abrasive at first, then completely absorbing. Give it two full listens before deciding.
Do you want energy in the background or something neutral?
branch.fork: Energy — keep the morning moving
Neutral — just fill the space without intruding

branch.content: DJ Aphrodite melodic mix or DJ Marky FabricLive 55. Melodic DnB and liquid breaks that feel upbeat without demanding your attention. The kind of music that improves whatever you are doing without you noticing.
Luomo or Clams Casino instrumentals. Both sit at low volume without losing their quality. Interesting enough to pay attention to if you choose, quiet enough to completely ignore if you do not.
Winding down or still in the middle of the evening?
branch.fork: Winding down — the evening is ending
Still going — plenty of evening left

branch.content: Liquid DnB or something softer?
branch.fork: Liquid DnB — I still want rhythm, just calmer
Softer — I am genuinely winding down

branch.content: Calibre, Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35, or Commix. All three sit in that space between energy and calm — still rhythmic, still moving, but nothing that will keep you up. Commix if you want the most reflective of the three.
Luomo or Four Tet FabricLive 59. Both have enough rhythm to feel alive but enough space to let your mind slow down. Four Tet if you want something with a bit more melodic warmth.
Getting ready to go out, or happily settled in?
branch.fork: Getting ready to go out — build the energy up
Settled in — enjoying the evening at home

branch.content: Build it in stages. Start with Aphrodite melodic or DJ Marky. Move to Deekline and Ed Solo or DJ Craze when you are nearly ready. By the time you leave you will already be in the right state.
Seba mixes or Autonomic era DnB — FabricLive 50. Half-step rhythm that is perfectly calibrated for an evening in. Engaging without being demanding. Exactly the right balance.
Alone or still with people?
branch.fork: Alone — just me and the headphones
Still with people but the evening is quieting down

branch.content: Introspective or floating — do you want to think, or disappear?
branch.fork: Introspective — I want music that matches where my head is
Floating — I want to completely let go

branch.content: Clams Casino instrumentals or The Bug. Both sit in a dark, searching space without being oppressive. Clams Casino if you want something hazy and soft. The Bug if you want something with more weight and friction.

LTJ Bukem — Logical Progression or FabricLive 46. Atmospheric DnB designed to dissolve the room around you. Or FabricLive 50 Autonomic if you want something with a bit more tension underneath the surface.
Still some energy in the room, or genuinely winding down?
branch.fork: Still energy — people might dance in the kitchen
Genuinely winding down — the night is nearly done

branch.content: DJ Q FabricLive 99 or the Speed Garage mix. Late night UKG has exactly the right energy for this — groove-heavy, not aggressive, keeps things moving without forcing it.
Amon Tobin or Four Tet FabricLive 59. Both fill a room beautifully at low volume. Complex enough to hold attention if anyone is still listening, easy enough to talk over if they are not.
What kind of work?
branch.fork: Creative work — writing, designing, making
Analytical or logical — thinking, problem-solving
Repetitive or mechanical — just need to get through it

branch.content: Do lyrics pull your attention away from what you are doing?
branch.fork: Yes — lyrics always distract me
No — I can filter them out

branch.content: Do you want texture and atmosphere, or steady rhythm?
branch.fork: Texture and atmosphere — music as environment
Steady rhythm — something that keeps me in flow

branch.content: Amon Tobin or Clams Casino instrumentals. Both create a sonic environment rather than a foreground. Your brain fills in the gaps creatively. Genuinely useful for creative work, not just pleasant background noise.

Seba mixes or Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35. The half-step roll of this kind of DnB keeps you moving at exactly the right pace for sustained focus. Consistent enough to not interrupt, rhythmic enough to keep you from drifting.
Do you want energy or something that just keeps you comfortable?
branch.fork: Energy — the work benefits from it
Comfortable — I just need to stay in it

branch.content: De La Soul. Energising without being aggressive, lyrical without being distracting once you know the tracks. Stakes Is High or Stakes Is High for sustained creative sessions.
DJ Aphrodite melodic or the 2010 mix. Warm, flowing DnB that keeps the temperature right without ever demanding you pay attention to it. Steady company for a long work session.
Do you need near silence around the music, or can it be more present?
branch.fork: Near silence — minimal, unobtrusive, almost ambient
More present is fine — I work better with something to push against

branch.content: Luomo or FabricLive 50 Autonomic. Both are almost ambient in their restraint. Enough rhythm to keep you from feeling the silence, sparse enough to completely disappear behind whatever you are thinking about.

LTJ Bukem — FabricLive 46 or Logical Progression. Atmospheric DnB at a level where it fills the room without dominating it. Complex enough to feel like good company, controlled enough to never pull you out of your work.
Do you want music to push you through it, or to make it actually enjoyable?
branch.fork: Push me through it — I need momentum
Make it enjoyable — I want to look forward to the session

branch.content: DJ Aphrodite or the Freestylers. High energy, consistent pace, no slow moments. The kind of music that removes the option of slowing down. Put it on loud and just go.

Pick a FabricLive mix you have not heard yet and treat it as a reward for sitting down to do the work. Something new keeps you engaged without you having to think about it. FabricLive 44 Commix or FabricLive 55 DJ Marky are good starting points.
